input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its main hardware layers comprise lower and upper chassis levels together with autonomy elements (perception, planning, control). The vehicle runs Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) on resource‑constrained electronic control units (ECUs), operating in an automotive driving domain, which makes it a prime candidate for digital twinning to off‑load computation and enable remote management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface that streams real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and provides capabilities to view, save, and generate reports of the collected data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle identification and desired‑speed computation) is executed in the DT (cloud) and the resulting speed set‑point is transmitted back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI allows operators to enable/disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency‑brake command. These services target two user groups: operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting with data collection).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires clock synchronization between physical and digital components with an accuracy of 100 ms, as mandated by safety‑critical vehicular standards; devices share a common notion of time to enable computation offloading.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin comprises three hierarchical entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each instantiated once and mapped onto separate hardware components; each entity contains one or more functional entities (FE) such as ROS2 nodes, forming a decentralized but coordinated structure.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, organizing software components into entity‑based functional entities that are implemented as individual ROS2 packages; each package hosts ROS2 nodes that realize the FE, enabling integration and resource management across the three entities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. Computational functionality is organized into software components that are mapped to Functional Entities (FEs) and implemented as ROS2 packages. Within the Digital Twin Entity, data‑management components such as the Data Storage module handle persistence of model data. The Physical Twin Entity hosts general automotive processing, notably a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking block that consumes raw sensor point‑cloud streams and produces higher‑level geometric representations. Data pre‑processing, actuation logic and device‑communication functions are also encapsulated in FEs. No explicit geometric, behavioral or analytical model descriptions are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The documents state that actuation functionality is encapsulated within the Physical Twin Entity and is provided by a Functional Entity that implements actuation of the OME. This FE is implemented as a ROS 2 package and interacts with the physical actuators of the system, but the specific actuator types, control interfaces, actuation ranges, safety constraints, or addressing mechanisms are not described in the provided texts.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: According to the documents, sensing capabilities are part of the Physical Twin Entity, which hosts the sensors required for the twin. The sensing infrastructure is represented by Functional Entities that are mapped to ROS 2 packages, but the texts do not enumerate concrete sensor models, measurement specifications, spatial arrangement, sampling rates, transmission bandwidth, or accuracy/precision figures.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the Physical Twin (LiDAR point clouds, motor state, steering servo position, vehicle state) is streamed to the Digital Twin through a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component and a Data Pre‑Processing FE. The Data Translation FE (ISO 23247 Data Translation) serialises the streams, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Inter‑component communication on the DT side uses a ROS 2 middleware network, while the physical‑to‑virtual link employs a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. The data types transmitted include raw point clouds, object tracks, vehicle state information, and projected driving paths, which are pre‑processed (clustering, tracking, unit conversion) before integration into the DT.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin issues control commands back to the Physical Twin via the Actuation FE. Commands include ACC enable/disable, desired speed set‑points, and emergency braking signals, which are consumed by the vehicle’s actuation modules (motor, steering servo). The messages are formatted according to the Data Translation FE specifications and transmitted over the same Wi‑Fi / UDP link. The vehicle acknowledges command execution through updated vehicle state feedback displayed in the 3D visualization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑entity communication between the physical twin and the DTS uses a Wi‑Fi network with UDP transport. Internally, DTS software components communicate over a ROS 2 middleware network. The architecture is designed to meet low end‑to‑end latency requirements needed for safety‑critical ACC operation, but the documents do not specify exact bandwidth, latency values, redundancy mechanisms, or security protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: ACC computation is offloaded from the resource‑constrained vehicle to the Digital Twin hosted on cloud resources, providing higher‑performance compute for the ACC algorithm. The DTS includes UI components for monitoring, data collection, reporting, and remote management, implying a cloud‑based deployment model with sufficient storage for vehicle state logs and scalability to handle additional data streams. Availability is addressed by the requirement for low‑latency communication, but explicit redundancy or fault‑tolerance mechanisms are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 reference‑architecture guidance. During the architectural design phase the developers first enumerate software components (see Table I) and define their relationships to sensors/actuators of the physical twin. Each software component is then mapped to a Functional Entity (FE) defined by the standard and implemented as an individual ROS 2 package. A ROS 2 package may contain one or more ROS 2 nodes that realize the FE’s functionality (e.g., data acquisition, preprocessing, actuation). The mapping organises the code according to the three ISO entities: the User Entity (User Interface FE – 3‑D visualization and control‑panel components), the Digital Twin Entity (data‑management and control mechanisms), and the Physical Twin Entity (device communication, point‑cloud clustering, tracking). The design deliberately departs from the standard by introducing a dedicated Physical Twin Entity to host compute‑resource functionality. Versioning and evolution are managed implicitly by the ROS 2 package ecosystem, but the documents do not detail explicit version‑control or QA practices.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin provides decision‑support through three main capabilities: (1) Data Monitoring – the user interface allows operators to view live vehicle state data, save snapshots, and generate reports for performance analysis; (2) Computation Offloading – the DT executes Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) logic, determining the lead vehicle and computing a desired speed, thereby reducing the computational load on the vehicle's primary control units; (3) Remote Management – operators can remotely enable or disable ACC, adjust the target speed, and issue emergency braking commands. These functions combine real‑time data acquisition, analytics, and command generation, enabling users to make informed control decisions about the physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is built to comply with the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing (parts 1–4). The reference architecture is adopted as the primary guideline for defining entities and functional entities. For inter‑device communication the design can employ OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as suggested by the standard. Runtime components are realised as ROS 2 packages, which rely on the DDS (Data Distribution Service) specification for publish‑subscribe communication, providing interoperability with other DDS‑compliant tools. The implementation respects vehicular‑safety clock‑synchronisation requirements of ≤100 ms accuracy, a constraint referenced as a typical mandate of safety‑critical vehicular standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted description is highly specific and aligns perfectly with the detailed description of the physical twin in Section III.B and the context in Section III.A. It correctly identifies the scale, base vehicle, chassis layers, and the motivation for digital twinning. Supported by quotes: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'built off a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle [3] by retrofitting it with autonomy components', and 'DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction provides a comprehensive and detailed summary of the three core services defined in Section III.A. It accurately captures the functionality of Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management, as well as the target user groups. Supported by quotes: '1) Data Monitoring: ... UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports ... real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', '2) Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT... sending the desired speed back to the vehicle', and 'The users of our system can be grouped into two classes: operators and developers'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly details the software development kit, middleware, programming languages, and simulation tools used in the implementation. Quotes: 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'Python 3.8, C++, MATLAB/Simulink'.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the 100 ms synchronization accuracy and the rationale for computation offloading (devices sharing a common notion of time). However, it inaccurately states that 100 ms is 'mandated by safety-critical vehicular standards,' whereas the text clarifies that standards mandate stricter limits and 100 ms is chosen due to hardware limitations. Quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms', 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.', 'Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization than 100 ms... We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The extraction accurately describes the three hierarchical entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User), their instantiation on separate hardware, and their composition of Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS2 nodes. Quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'Each entity is composed of Functional Entities (FEs)', 'Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality provided by the FE.'

The extraction precisely captures the use of the ISO 23247 reference architecture, the organization of components into entity-based FEs, and their implementation as individual ROS2 packages for integration. Quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase', 'organize the SW components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247', 'we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package.'

The source text focuses on the internal architecture and services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) of the specific ACC Digital Twin. It does not describe integration with external systems, other Digital Twins, or enterprise systems, which aligns with the 'Not in Document' extraction.

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data

_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the ISO 23247-based architecture, the mapping of software components to Functional Entities (FEs), and their implementation as ROS2 packages. It correctly identifies the Data Storage module in the Digital Twin Entity and the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking block (which processes LiDAR data) in the Physical Twin Entity. It also notes the absence of specific geometric/behavioral model descriptions, which is consistent with the text's focus on architecture over detailed simulation modeling. Supported by quotes: 'we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN', and 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'.

The extraction accurately notes that actuation is encapsulated in the Physical Twin Entity and implemented as a ROS2 package. It correctly states that specific details like actuator types or control interfaces are not described in the text. The source mentions 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' and 'Actuation FE', but lacks further hardware specifics. The extraction is well-grounded but limited by the source's brevity on physical actuators.

The extraction correctly identifies sensing capabilities as part of the Physical Twin Entity and their representation by Functional Entities mapped to ROS2 packages. It accurately points out the absence of detailed sensor specifications in the text. The source lists sensors like 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera' but does not provide measurement specs or spatial arrangements. The extraction is accurate but constrained by the source's limited detail on sensing infrastructure.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the abstract defines digital twins generally as 'real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections', the specific case study requirements (R1-R8) and implementation details focus on architecture, communication latency, and clock synchronization rather than model fidelity requirements, validation/verification methods, or uncertainty quantification. The provided text does not contain information specific to these characteristic definitions.

_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than

To implement these services, the DT must support low

respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

I) and their relationships to each other', 'To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', and 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA'. However, the claims about versioning being 'managed implicitly by the ROS 2 package ecosystem' and that 'the documents do not detail explicit version-control or QA practices' are not explicitly supported by the source text.

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate summary of the decision-support capabilities described in Section III.A. It correctly identifies Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading (ACC logic), and Remote Management (ACC control, speed adjustment, emergency braking) as the three main capabilities. It includes specific technical details like the determination of the lead vehicle and computation of desired speed. Supported quotes: 'The DT presented in this case will contain a user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The description accurately captures the standardization aspects of the system and is well-supported by the source text. Key quotes include: 'Currently, the only DT-specific architectural standard is the relatively new ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework', 'Part 2 of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA designed and developed for manufacturing systems', 'we draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase', 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware to integrate application modules', and 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms'.

Oct. 2018.

[36] 3. G. P. Project, “Service requirements for v2x services – technical
specification,” European Telecommunications Standards Institute, Tech.
Rep. 3GPP TS 22.185 version 15.0.0 Release 15, Jul. 2018.

[37] M. Dalibor, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, S. Varga, and A. Wortmann, “Towards a model-driven architecture for interactive digital twin cockpits,”
in _Conceptual Modeling: 39th International Conference_, Springer, 2020,
pp. 377–387. DOI [: 10.1007/978-3-030-62522-1 28](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-62522-1_28)

[38] International Organization for Standardization (ISO), “Road vehicles –
Functional safety,” Tech. Rep. ISO 26262-1:2018, Dec. 2018.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
